title: Binary Search Exact Match
description: Given a sorted array and a target value, print its index or -1 if it is absent.
outputFormat: Print the index of target or -1
intendedApproachSummary: Compare the target with the middle value and keep only the half that can still contain it.
wrongApproachHints: A linear scan works but misses the main binary-search idea.
The array must stay sorted for this logic to work.
hints: Use left and right pointers.
Compute mid carefully.

title: Lower Bound
description: Given a sorted array and a target, print the first index where the value is at least the target. If no such index exists, print n.
intendedApproachSummary: When arr[mid] is large enough, record mid as an answer candidate and continue left.
wrongApproachHints: Do not stop at the first matching value you see unless you know it is the first valid one.
hints: Track an answer variable initialized to n.
Move right only when arr[mid] < target.

examples.explanation: Index 2 is the first position with value at least 4.

title: First and Last Occurrence
description: Given a sorted array and target x, print the first and last index of x, or -1 -1 if x is absent.
outputFormat: Print two integers: first occurrence and last occurrence
intendedApproachSummary: Use one boundary search for the first occurrence and another for the last occurrence.
wrongApproachHints: Do not expand linearly after finding one copy.
Left and right boundaries need slightly different conditions.
hints: Find the first index with value >= x.
Find the first index with value > x, then step back.

examples.explanation: 2 starts at index 1 and ends at index 3.

title: Search Insert Position
description: Given a sorted array and target x, print the index where x should be inserted to keep the array sorted.
intendedApproachSummary: This is exactly the lower-bound position of x.
wrongApproachHints: Do not return -1 when the target is absent.
The answer is still valid even if x already exists.
hints: Think in terms of the first index where value >= x.

title: Search in Rotated Sorted Array
description: Given a sorted array rotated at an unknown pivot and a target, print the target index or -1 if absent.
intendedApproachSummary: At each step one half is sorted. Decide whether the target belongs to that sorted half or the other half.
wrongApproachHints: The whole array is not globally sorted anymore, so plain comparisons are not enough.
hints: Check whether left..mid is sorted or mid..right is sorted.
Then test whether the target lies inside that sorted interval.

title: Minimum in Rotated Sorted Array
description: Given a rotated sorted array with distinct values, print the minimum value.
intendedApproachSummary: Compare nums[mid] with nums[right] to decide which side still contains the minimum.
wrongApproachHints: Do not scan linearly for the minimum.
The right end comparison is often the cleanest signal.
hints: If nums[mid] <= nums[right], the minimum is at mid or to the left.

title: Find Peak Element
description: Given an array where adjacent elements are never equal, print the index of any peak element.
outputFormat: Print the index of any peak
intendedApproachSummary: If nums[mid] < nums[mid + 1], a peak exists to the right; otherwise a peak exists to the left or at mid.
wrongApproachHints: You are not searching for an exact value.
The slope direction tells you where a peak must exist.
hints: Compare mid with mid + 1.
Use a left < right style loop.

testCases.explanation: Index 5 is one valid peak for this case.

title: Floor Square Root
description: Given a non-negative integer x, print floor(sqrt(x)).
intendedApproachSummary: Binary search the integer answer range [0, x] and keep the largest mid whose square is <= x.
wrongApproachHints: Be careful about overflow when computing mid * mid.
Do not return the first square larger than x.
hints: Use long for multiplication.
Track the best valid answer so far.

examples.explanation: 4^2 = 16 and 5^2 = 25, so the floor square root is 4.

title: Koko Eating Bananas
description: Given piles of bananas and h hours, print the minimum integer speed Koko needs so that she finishes within h hours.
intendedApproachSummary: Binary search the speed. For each speed, compute total hours needed and test if it is feasible.
wrongApproachHints: The speed is the search space, not the array index.
The feasibility function must be monotonic.
hints: Low can start at 1.
High can start at the maximum pile size.

title: Ship Packages Within D Days
description: Given package weights and D days, print the minimum ship capacity needed to deliver all packages in order within D days.
edgeCases: Capacity must be at least the heaviest package
intendedApproachSummary: Binary search the capacity and simulate how many days that capacity requires.
wrongApproachHints: The answer is not an index in the array.
Your helper should count days greedily in order.
hints: Low starts at max(weight).
High starts at sum(weight).

examples.explanation: Capacity 3 is enough to ship in 3 days.

title: Minimum Days to Make Bouquets
description: Given bloom days, m bouquets, and k adjacent flowers per bouquet, print the minimum day needed, or -1 if impossible.
outputFormat: Print the minimum feasible day or -1
intendedApproachSummary: Binary search the day and greedily count how many bouquets can be formed by that day.
wrongApproachHints: The feasibility helper should reset the consecutive flower count when a flower has not bloomed yet.
hints: If m * k > n, answer is immediately -1.
Search days between min and max bloom day.

examples.explanation: By day 3, flowers at days 1, 3, and 2 are ready.

title: Median of Two Sorted Arrays
description: Given two sorted arrays, print the median of the combined sorted order.
outputFormat: Print the median as a decimal if needed
intendedApproachSummary: Binary search the partition position in the smaller array so that left partitions contain the correct number of elements and all left values are <= all right values.
wrongApproachHints: Do not merge the arrays fully.
Always binary search the smaller array for cleaner boundaries.
hints: Define partitions cut1 and cut2.
Use sentinel values for empty sides.

examples.explanation: The merged order is [1, 2, 3], so the median is 2.
